commonIssues: Container minimum (~19 L) reflects RHS guidance of a pot at least 30 cm (12 in) wide and deep for one plant; choose a compact cultivar (e.g. 'Dwarf Green Curled') for container culture.
Bolts and turns bitter in summer heat; best grown as a cool-season spring and fall/autumn crop. Frost actually sweetens the leaves.
Susceptible to cabbage-family pests (cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles) as a Brassica.
Keep soil consistently moist (about 1 inch / 25 mm of water per week); sandy soils need more frequent watering.

source.citation: UMN Extension — Growing collards and kale in home gardens: watering '1 inch of rainfall per week is good', sandy soils need more frequent watering.

UMN Extension — Growing collards and kale: feeding when plants reach ~4 in tall, e.g. 3-1/2 cups 10-3-1 per 100 ft of row; cold tolerant and keeps producing well beyond first fall frosts; neutral soil pH 6.0 to 7.5 ideal.
UMN Extension — Growing collards and kale: neutral soil pH 6.0 to 7.5 ideal; loamy, well-drained soil with high organic matter.
Clemson HGIC — 'Kale Is a Headless Cabbage': needs at least six hours of sun; germinates at soil temps 40-85 F; grows well above 50 F; leaves hardy to 20 F (about -7 C).
Clemson HGIC — 'Kale Is a Headless Cabbage': leaves hardy to 20 F (-6.7 C, rounded to -7 C); grows well when temperatures stay above 50 F.

RHS — How to grow Kale: use a large container at least 30 cm (12 in) wide and deep for one plant; choose compact cultivars for containers; container plants need regular watering; feeding usually only needed on poor soils or in containers (nitrogen-rich feed).
RHS — How to grow Kale: hardy leafy vegetable cropping from September onwards through the coldest months; likes sun but copes in light shade.

Old Farmer's Almanac — Kale: cool-weather crop, plant 4-6 weeks before last spring frost and ~3 months before first fall frost; continues growing until ~20 F (-7 C); flavor improves after a light frost; can turn bitter in summer heat.

Confidence note: heatStressC (27 C / ~80 F) is a conservative estimate. Sources (RHS, Almanac) state kale turns bitter and is prone to bolting in summer heat/strong sun but give no single numeric heat-stress threshold; hotWeatherMultiplier and feeding baseIntervalDays are also derived/estimated from the qualitative '1 inch/week' and per-row fertilizer guidance rather than published container intervals.
